rulings.Case Title: ALFONSO PAGANO JR ET AL VS. AKEBONO BRAKE CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Motion For Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: On Asbestos Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, March 11, 2025 in Department 304, Line 1. 1. Plaintiffs' Discovery Responses Contrary to Defendant's assertion, Plaintiffs' discovery responses are not factually devoid. Rather, they contain detailed facts regarding Plaintiff's exposure. Plaintiff identified the presence of Defendant's workers on the USS Kitty Hawk while it was dry docked at Hunters Point Naval Shipyard, described their activities, and stated that they removed, installed, and cleaned up refractory materials in Plaintiff's presence. (Defense Exhibit D at 2:3-3:2.) These responses are specific and not boilerplate allegations. (See Ganoe v. Metalclad Insulation Corp. (2014) 227 Cal.App.4th 1577, 1584 [reversing summary judgment for defendant where plaintiffs' amended discovery responses cited to specific facts linking defendant to the decedent's exposure to asbestos, including identifying decedent's coworker as a product identification witness].) Moreover, Plaintiffs' responses identify the specific asbestos-containing products and the insulation and refractory materials that Defendant's employees worked with that contained asbestos through at least the 1970's. (Defense Exhibit D at 2:3-3:2; 4:4-5-11:3.) (See Ramirez v. Avon Products, Inc. (2023) 87 Cal.App.5th 939, 955.) As the opposition states, Defendant does not address any of that evidence or attempt to refute any of these facts in its motion. Since Plaintiffs have provided factually rich discovery responses, Defendant cannot rely on the absence of other evidence to shift the burden. "A motion for summary judgment is not a mechanism for rewarding limited discovery; it is a mechanism allowing the early disposition of cases where there is no reason to believe that a party will be able to prove its case." (Weber v. John Crane, Inc. (2006) 143 Cal.App.4th 1433, 1442 [reversing summary judgment where defendant "produced no evidence allowing an inference that plaintiffs neither possess, nor reasonably can obtain, any evidence that [decedent] was exposed to an asbestos-containing product manufactured or supplied by [defendant]"]; see also Scheiding v. Dinwiddie Constr. Co. (1999) 69 Cal.App.4th 64, 75-75.) The Court notes Defendant relies on the declaration of Defendant's Safety & Risk Management Advisor, Doyle Tyree, to argue that no records exist showing Defendant performed work on the USS Kitty Hawk, or at any other location, including the USS Kitty Hawk's homeport, North Island Naval Air Station in San Diego/Coronado. (Motion at 11.) However, as stated above, Mr. Tyree's declaration lacks foundation and constitutes inadmissible hearsay. (Evid. Code 702(a).) Moreover, the lack of business records is insufficient to establish that no work was performed, absent evidence that such records were regularly maintained and complete, which is lacking here. (Evid. Code 1272; see, e.g., People v. Dickinson (1976) 59 Cal.App.3d 314, 318-319.) Even if Defendant had met its burden, Plaintiffs' opposition evidence raises triable issues of fact. Defendant argues that other unrelated "Thorpe" entities could have performed the work on the USS Kitty Hawk. (Motion at 11.) However, Defendant fails to present evidence that any other Thorpe entity operated in the same industry, during the relevant time (i.e.,1972-1973), and in Northern California. Plaintiffs' evidence, the deposition of Defendant's President John T. Dawson, shows that Defendant performed similar marine refractory work in Northern California, including on ships that were used in war efforts, like the USS Kitty Hawk. (PAMF Nos. 11, 13, and 14.) Given this evidence, a reasonable juror could conclude that Defendant's workers were responsible for Plaintiff's asbestos exposure. Thus, Defendant's motion for summary judgment is denied. (Tentative ruling continued, see next entry)

rulings.Case Title: ALFONSO PAGANO JR ET AL VS. AKEBONO BRAKE CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT J.T. THORPE & SON, INC.'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT OR, IN THE ALTERNATIVE, MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION (ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR ISSUANCE OF TENTATIVE RULING ONLY)
rulings.Rulings: (PART 2 OF 2) (Tentative ruling continued from previous entry) Plaintiffs do not oppose Defendant's motions for summary adjudication of their Third Cause of Action for Negligent Misrepresentation and Fourth Cause of Action for Fraud by Nondisclosure, which are GRANTED. 1. Strict Liability Defendant's Motion for Summary Adjudication to Plaintiffs' Second Cause of Action for Strict Liability is DENIED. Defendant fails to submit evidence to satisfy its burden that Plaintiffs' discovery responses are factually devoid as to evidence of strict liability. As Plaintiffs correctly contend, Defendant fails to address any of the facts related to strict liability set forth in their discovery responses. (Defense Exhibit D at 3:21-4:4; 3:6-20; 13:4-15.) Defendant does not address these facts in its motion and has not provided sufficient evidence to carry its burden to establish a triable issue of material fact as to this cause of action. Accordingly, the motion for summary adjudication as to Plaintiffs' Second Cause of Action for strict liability is denied. 2. Punitive Damages Defendant's Motion for Summary Adjudication as to the claim for punitive damages is DENIED. Defendant again fails to meet its burden of demonstrating that Plaintiffs will be unable to make a showing that Defendant acted with malice, fraud, or oppression. (Civ. Code 3294, subd. (a).) Plaintiffs' discovery responses set forth how Defendant knew of asbestos hazards and yet nevertheless failed to warn Plaintiff and others who could be exposed to asbestos from the products Defendant supplied and materials Defendant worked with. (Defense Exhibit D at 11:21-12:16, 13:16-14:2.) Defendant fails to address any of these facts, submit any affirmative evidence refuting these facts, or otherwise demonstrate that Plaintiffs will be unable to prove a case for punitive damages at trial. Furthermore, Plaintiffs have provided evidence that Defendant was aware of the hazards of asbestos as early as 1960 but continued to use it in its products. (PAMF Nos. 22-23.) Defendant did nothing to control the asbestos hazards until the 1960s or early 1970s. (PAMF No. 23.) Accordingly, the motion for summary adjudication as to Plaintiffs' claim for punitive damages is denied. The moving party shall lodge with the clerk in Department 304 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling. Any party wishing to contest the tentative ruling must email contestasbestostr@sftc.org by 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing and state their intention to contest. If a hearing is requested, it will be on March 11, 2025, at 9:00 a.m. Attorneys may appear in person or remotely via zoom: Meeting ID 160 757 8308; Passcode: 485029. Face coverings are optional. The Court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law and Motion department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: Their name, CSR and telephone number, and their individual work email address. There will be only one official record. If the parties cannot agree, the Court will designate a qualified court reporter to provide the official transcript for the matter, and the party or parties will bear the cost. = (304/EPS)
